jokes.punchline: Jerry's vehement denial of the airport prediction becomes a running defensive mantra
jokes.explanation: The airport denial works as a running gag because it's a litmus test for how far friendship exploitation goes. Jerry's vehement 'I'm not driving him to the airport!' becomes funnier each time it's repeated because the audience suspects the progression is inevitable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about spit. It's a fascinating thing. The trajectory, the velocity, the angle of the jaw... I mean, if you really break it down scientifically, spit travels at approximately 40 miles per hour when it leaves the mouth at a 45-degree angle. That's faster than most people can run. So technically, if someone spits on you, you've been assaulted by a biological projectile moving at near-highway speeds.

jokes.text: Carrie's breakup speech: 'It's over, buddy. Done. Finished. So long. Goodbye. Adios. Sayonara.'
jokes.setup: Carrie calls George to break up with him
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire succession of synonyms for 'goodbye' as a breakup is absurdly comprehensive
jokes.explanation: The exhaustive list of farewells is funny in its completeness. 'Adios. Sayonara' capping it with multilingual options is the right escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The magic loogie!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're not gonna sneeze on me, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm not sick. I just have something in my throat.

jokes.text: Carrie dumps George: 'You got no job. You got no prospects. You're like Biff Loman.'
jokes.setup: George has been unemployed and job-hunting (fraudulently); Carrie has been patient
jokes.punchline: The 'Biff Loman' reference — from Death of a Salesman, the tragic figure of a man who never achieved his potential — applied to George perfectly
jokes.explanation: 'You're like Biff Loman' is a genuinely sharp literary reference that lands because it's precisely correct. The callback to 'Biff wants to be a buff' from earlier (using 'Biff' in George's universe) is an additional layer. Carrie using Death of a Salesman as a breakup weapon is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Jerry, it was a magic loogie.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A magic loogie? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The trajectory was impossible. It curved mid-air, hit the building, bounced back. Just like the magic bullet.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now we're using the Kennedy assassination to explain your spit?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's the only explanation!

jokes.text: George: 'I went to the hardware store interview.' / Carrie: 'You think I'm going to spend my life with somebody because he can get me a deal on a box of nails?'
jokes.setup: George defends his job-seeking by mentioning the hardware store
jokes.punchline: Carrie's devastating deflation: 'because he can get me a deal on a box of nails?'
jokes.explanation: A great callback to the hardware store bit. Carrie's line reduces George's 'fascinating' hardware to the most prosaic possible item (a box of nails) as the basis for their relationship. The specificity of 'nails' over any other hardware item is well chosen.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The shot came from the grassy knoll!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look at the head movement. Back and to the left. Back and to the left.

jokes.text: George: 'I thought we were a team.' / Carrie: 'If I ever need a drill bit, I'll call you.'
jokes.setup: George tries to appeal to their relationship
jokes.punchline: Carrie continues the hardware metaphor — the only thing George is good for is getting her a drill bit
jokes.explanation: Sustains the hardware metaphor through the breakup. 'If I ever need a drill bit, I'll call you' is a cold and funny dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this, and I've come to a conclusion. There's no way one person could have done this alone. There had to be a second gunman. Look at the angles here. It's impossible.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, the Warren Commission got it all wrong. There's a grassy knoll involved in this somehow.

jokes.text: George: 'Carrie, could you do me a favor? Could you not mention this to your mother?'
jokes.setup: Carrie has just brutally broken up with George
jokes.punchline: George's primary concern post-breakup is protecting his chance at getting Mrs. Sokol's daughter's number — a completely self-serving and revealing non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: A sublime George moment. His only concern after being devastatingly dumped is whether his ex-girlfriend will tell her mother, which would ruin his shot at the daughter's number. This encapsulates George's character completely — petty, self-interested, and weirdly strategic. Excellent economy and character integration. One of the episode's best character beats.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: There was a second spitter!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's a conspiracy. Think about it. You're on the street, someone spits on you, you look around — nobody's there. How'd they do it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe they ran away.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. They had to be positioned on the grassy knoll.

jokes.text: Keith brags to Elaine: 'I hate to brag, but I did win 11 straight Gold Gloves. I wouldn't have brought it up, but since you mentioned it.' / Elaine: 'I didn't mention it.'
jokes.setup: Keith and Elaine are on their date
jokes.punchline: Keith claims he 'hates to brag' and only brought up the 11 Gold Gloves because Elaine 'mentioned it' — she didn't
jokes.explanation: Keith Hernandez's self-aggrandizement delivered with false modesty ('I hate to brag... since you mentioned it') is a great character moment. Elaine's flat 'I didn't mention it' is perfect. Humanizes the celebrity by revealing his vanity.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'Well, you played first base. I mean, they always put the worst player at first base. That's where they put me, and I stunk.'
jokes.setup: Keith is bragging about his Gold Gloves at first base
jokes.punchline: Elaine dismisses first base as where they put the worst player — then reveals her evidence is that's where she was put in childhood
jokes.explanation: Elaine's logic — first base is where you put the worst player, because that's where she was put — is great. The self-deprecation that inadvertently deflates Keith's achievement is well constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've come to the conclusion that it was not a spit take. It was a sneeze that became a cough that became a wheeze that ended in a spit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But I'm treating it like the Kennedy assassination. There's the official report, and then there's what really happened.

jokes.text: Well, so what? I mean, you played first base. I mean, they always put the worst player at first base. That's where they put me, and I stunk.
jokes.setup: Elaine pushes back on Keith's Gold Glove pride
jokes.punchline: 'That's where they put me, and I stunk' — Elaine's argument that first base is for bad players is undermined by her evidence: herself
jokes.explanation: Elaine's argument against Keith's Gold Gloves inadvertently proves his point — she's his evidence that first base is where bad players go, because that's where SHE played, and she stunk. The self-own is perfectly constructed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm starting a fake company. I need a phone number.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm using Jerry's number. He doesn't know it yet.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: When they call, Jerry will think it's for him. It's perfect.

jokes.text: Keith: 'Elaine, you don't know the first thing about first base.' / Elaine: 'Well, I know something about getting to first base... and I know you'll never be there.'
jokes.setup: Keith protests that Elaine knows nothing about first base
jokes.punchline: Elaine turns 'first base' into sexual baseball metaphor — and tells Keith he'll never 'get to first base' with her
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed double-meaning joke. The pivot from literal baseball first base to romantic 'first base' is smooth and Elaine's delivery as a put-down ('you'll never be there') makes it land well. The wordplay is genuinely earned.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a real estate company. We sell houses.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What's the name?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Pendant Publishing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Pendant Publishing? That's a publishing company.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no. It's real estate now. We changed it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: When did you change it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just now. I'm telling you right now.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So what do you do there?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm an architect.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: An architect? But you don't know anything about buildings.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm very good at it.

jokes.text: Elaine: 'Well, I know something about getting to first base. And I know you'll never be there.' / Keith: 'The way I figure it, I've already been there. And I plan on rounding second tonight at around 11:00.'
jokes.setup: They're sparring about first base in baseball
jokes.punchline: The baseball metaphor pivots to sexual innuendo — Keith is confident he's 'already been to first base' and plans to 'round second' at 11pm
jokes.explanation: The baseball/sex metaphor is executed with perfect structure — it emerges naturally from the conversation and escalates elegantly. Keith's specific '11 o'clock' timing is a great confident detail. Elaine's comeback about the third-base coach ('I don't think he's waving you in') is an excellent riposte.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So we're manufacturing the bra in my apartment?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, initially, yes. We're keeping overhead low.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: How are we gonna manufacture bras in my apartment? I don't have the equipment!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's why I'm the president and you're in manufacturing. I sell, you make.

jokes.text: Keith: 'The way I figure it, I've already been there. And I plan on rounding second tonight at around 11:00.' / Elaine: 'I'd watch the third-base coach if I were you, because I don't think he's waving you in.'
jokes.setup: The baseball-as-romance metaphor exchange continues
jokes.punchline: Keith extends the metaphor (already on base, heading to second); Elaine counters with the third-base coach not waving him home — i.e., she's not letting him score
jokes.explanation: The extended baseball metaphor for romance continues to develop well. 'The third-base coach isn't waving you in' is a sophisticated extension of the metaphor. The whole exchange (jokes 69-71) is one of the better dialogue sequences in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So you'll do it?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No? What do you mean, no? You always do things for me.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not this time.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But I really need you on this one, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The answer is no, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Come on, it'll only take five minutes.

jokes.text: Keith: 'You know, I hate to say this, but I think we're really hitting it off. Get it? Get it?' / Elaine: 'Clever.'
jokes.setup: The baseball-romance exchange is winding down
jokes.punchline: Keith makes the 'hitting it off' / baseball 'hit' pun and immediately points it out twice — Elaine's flat 'Clever' is the real punchline
jokes.explanation: The 'hitting it off' pun is the weakest of the baseball wordplay sequence, but saved by Keith calling it out ('Get it? Get it?') and Elaine's supremely dry 'Clever.' The meta-acknowledgment that the pun was obvious makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about getting into the latex business.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Latex? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm gonna manufacture fake latex. It's a gold mine! But let me tell you, the overhead is killing me. Do you know what my shipping costs are?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't have a latex business.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not yet. But when I do, I'm telling you, it's gonna be rough. The unions, the regulations... I mean, who knew fake latex was so complicated?

jokes.text: You know, I hate to say this, but I really think that we're hitting it off. / Get it? Get it? / Clever.
jokes.setup: Keith continues the baseball metaphor into conversation about their date
jokes.punchline: Keith's 'hitting it off' — then immediately explaining the baseball pun ('Get it? Get it?'); Elaine's dry 'Clever.'
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy about bad puns. Keith pointing out his own pun with 'Get it? Get it?' and Elaine's flat 'Clever' is self-aware joke commentary. The pun is acknowledged as slightly too obvious — which is the joke. Good button on the baseball flirtation sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: So what do you want to do tonight?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know, maybe we could see a movie?

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: JFK? I heard that's really good.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: JFK? I just did JFK!

jokes.text: Keith pulls out a cigarette. Elaine: 'You smoke?' / Keith: 'Is that a problem?' — long uncomfortable pause
jokes.setup: The date has been going wonderfully well
jokes.punchline: Keith reveals he smokes — a dealbreaker — and the comfortable silence of the date is shattered by an awkward, loaded pause
jokes.explanation: The smoking revelation is a great cringe beat — everything going well, then the dealbreaker appears. The visual of the ashtray callback from the coffee shop scene compounds it (Elaine said she hates cigarettes). The silence is the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Keith invites me to spend the weekend at his place, and I'm thinking, 'Great, finally someone wants to hang out with me.' Then I realize - he probably just needs help moving furniture or something.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the thing about me. I'm never the first choice. I'm like the emotional equivalent of a tax return - nobody's excited to see me, but everyone needs me around April.

jokes.text: Jerry's internal monologue/confession: 'She likes him. I mean, she really likes him. Who wouldn't like him? I like him, and I'm a guy.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is processing that Elaine genuinely likes Keith
jokes.punchline: Jerry's reasoning collapses on itself — 'I like him, and I'm a guy' — inadvertently proving his own feelings for Keith
jokes.explanation: 'I like him, and I'm a guy' — Jerry using his own fondness for Keith as evidence of Keith's attractiveness to Elaine, while also inadvertently admitting his own man-crush. This perfectly captures the episode's central comedic theme and is delivered with complete sincerity. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking about the handshake. You know, it's such a weird thing. You meet someone, you put your hand in their hand, you shake it. Why? What are we doing? Are we checking if they're real? Making sure they're not a ghost?

jokes.text: She likes him. I mean, she really likes him. / How do you know? / Who wouldn't like him? I like him, and I'm a guy. / I suppose he's an attractive man. / Forget that. He's a ballplayer. MVP, 1979. I'm making wisecracks in some nightclub. This guy was in game six.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George discuss Elaine and Keith
jokes.punchline: Jerry returning to the 'wisecracks in a nightclub' self-deprecation — now internalizing George's earlier dismissal — and 'I like him, and I'm a guy' as both a joke and a genuine admission
jokes.explanation: Good callback to George's 'wisecracks in a nightclub' dismissal. Jerry has internalized the hierarchy. 'I like him, and I'm a guy' is an honest admission that plays as comedy. The self-deprecation is consistent with Jerry's character and the episode's themes.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: So what's the deal with Elaine? Is she available?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I've been seeing her.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: Oh really? How long?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A few months now.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: Hmm, well if things don't work out, let me know. I'd like to take a shot.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'I know this sounds arrogant, but I never thought she'd find someone she liked better than me... I guess I had my chance. And that's that. You know what I'd like to do? I'd really like to have sex with a tall woman. Like a giant. Like 6'5''.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is moping about Keith and Elaine; sentimental reflection about relationships
jokes.punchline: The abrupt pivot from emotional vulnerability to 'I'd really like to have sex with a giant' (6'5'') is a perfect tonal whiplash
jokes.explanation: The pivot from emotional sincerity to an utterly specific physical fantasy ('Like a giant. Like 6'5''') is a great Seinfeld tonal move. The specificity of 6'5'' rather than just 'tall' is what makes it funny. Jerry's complete subject change as emotional coping is very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: Can I ask you something? Would it be weird if I dated Elaine?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, no.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, go ahead.

jokes.text: George: 'What was the tallest woman you ever slept with?' / Jerry: 'I don't know, 6'3''.' / George: 'Wow! God! See, this is all I think about: sleeping with a giant. It's my life's ambition.'
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions wanting to sleep with a 6'5'' woman
jokes.punchline: George immediately follows up — he too wants this, it's his 'life's ambition.' The fact that this is apparently what George thinks about constantly and treats as a serious ambition is the joke
jokes.explanation: George latching onto Jerry's non-sequitur and claiming it as his own 'life's ambition' is perfect character comedy. George treating 'sleeping with a giant' as a worthy goal on par with Edison or Magellan sets up the next joke well.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what's the deal with Keith's handshake?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's weak. It's a very weak handshake. No confidence, no strength. When you shake a man's hand, you're making a statement about who you are as a person. Keith's handshake says he's unsure, he's tentative, he's not fully committed to the interaction.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'So I guess it's fair to say you've set different goals for yourself than, say, Thomas Edison, Magellan, these type of people.'
jokes.setup: George says sleeping with a giant is his 'life's ambition'
jokes.punchline: Jerry contextualizes this against Edison and Magellan — the greatest achievers in history vs. George's aspiration
jokes.explanation: Jerry's dry comparison of George's ambition to Edison and Magellan is perfectly calibrated. The phrase 'these type of people' at the end has a wonderful casual quality. Well-constructed deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So how was your date?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It wasn't a date.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What do you mean it wasn't a date? You went out, you had dinner...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was just two people going out to eat.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's a date, Jerry.

jokes.text: George: 'Magellan? You like Magellan?' / Jerry: 'My favorite explorer. Around the world. Come on.' / George: 'Who do you like?' / Jerry: 'De Soto.' / George: 'What did he do?' / Jerry: 'Discovered the Mississippi.' / George: 'Oh, like they wouldn't have found that anyway.'
jokes.setup: Jerry and George debate favorite explorers
jokes.punchline: George dismisses De Soto's discovery of the Mississippi with 'Oh, like they wouldn't have found that anyway' — the ultimate backhanded dismissal of a major historical achievement
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, like they wouldn't have found that anyway' is one of the episode's most quotable and original lines. The dismissive logic — the Mississippi is so big it would inevitably have been found — applied to a historical explorer creates a beautiful absurdist comedic moment. The conversation itself is wonderfully absurd (two grown men having passionate opinions about explorers mid-discussion of sex with giants). The line is genuinely original and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't make it tonight, I have to cancel.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cancel? We've had this planned for weeks!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I know, I'm sorry, something came up.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Something came up? I'm not a restaurant reservation, Elaine. You can't just cancel me.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, it's not that big a deal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not that big a deal? Do you know how many people would kill to spend an evening with me?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, your mother.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'I like de Soto.' / George: 'De Soto? What did he do?' / Jerry: 'Discovered the Mississippi.' / George: 'Oh, like they wouldn't have found that anyway.'
jokes.setup: Jerry says his favorite explorer is de Soto
jokes.punchline: George dismisses de Soto's discovery of the Mississippi River: 'Oh, like they wouldn't have found that anyway'
jokes.explanation: George's dismissal of the discovery of the Mississippi River ('like they wouldn't have found that anyway') is a perfectly constructed piece of breezy intellectual nihilism. The confidence of the delivery, the specific historical detail, and the casual deflation all combine perfectly. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're just jealous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Jealous? I'm not jealous. I've been a fan since before you even knew who they were. I have all their albums on vinyl. I went to three concerts in the '80s. I own a signed poster.

jokes.text: Jerry asks Kramer where he's going: 'I gotta meet Keith at my apartment at 3. I'm helping him move.' / Kramer: 'The guy asked you to help him move? Wow!'
jokes.setup: Kramer encounters Jerry leaving for the Keith moving job
jokes.punchline: Kramer's reaction — 'Wow!' — treats this as a major milestone, confirming the male-friendship-as-dating parallel
jokes.explanation: Kramer's 'Wow' reaction at the moving invitation confirms it as a culturally significant male-relationship milestone. Sets up the next extended bit about what helping someone move means.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're jealous of David Puddy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not jealous of David Puddy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes, you are. You're jealous because he's with me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not jealous of him being with you. I'm jealous of you being with him.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: There's a big difference!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, there really isn't.

jokes.text: Jerry panics at Keith's moving inventory — sectional couch (12 pieces), marble coffee table (3 inches thick, from Italy), convertible sofa — 'Stairs? There's no elevator? It's a brownstone. Three floors.'
jokes.setup: Jerry agreed to help Keith move, expecting a light job
jokes.punchline: Each new piece of furniture is worse than the last — culminating in a three-floor brownstone with no elevator
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured escalation sequence. Each new furniture detail adds to the horror: 12-piece sectional, marble (from Italy!), convertible sofa that opens up on stairs, no elevator, three floors. The rhythm of Jerry's growing dread, punctuated by 'Stairs?' is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hello, Pendant Publishing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yes, Mr. Pitt is expecting your call. One moment please.

jokes.text: Jerry breaks down: 'I can't do this. I can't! It's too soon. I don't know you. I can't help you move, I'm sorry. I can't! I just can't.'
jokes.setup: Keith reveals the full scope of the moving job
jokes.punchline: Jerry backs out using language straight out of a romantic 'too soon' rejection — 'It's too soon. I don't know you.'
jokes.explanation: The perfect payoff to the 'helping someone move = going all the way' metaphor. Jerry's rejection uses exact language from romantic refusals: 'It's too soon. I don't know you.' Applied to moving furniture, it's the episode's central joke paid off brilliantly. 'I can't! I just can't.' echoes every rom-com rejection scene. One of the episode's standout moments.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We gotta get over there right now. This is a time-sensitive situation. The baby's not gonna be a baby forever. In a few years, it's gonna be a kid. Then it's a teenager. You can't just show up whenever you feel like it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, it's been two days.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! We're already behind schedule. This is a critical window. We miss it, we're looking at a toddler. A toddler, Jerry!

jokes.text: Keith confronts Newman and Kramer; Kramer announces 'And then you spit on us!' — and Keith reveals it was actually Roger McDowell
jokes.setup: The JFK-spit mystery has been an unresolved thread; now Keith himself arrives
jokes.punchline: Keith's account resolves the second-spitter mystery: it was Roger McDowell, who was angry about them pouring beer on his head in the bullpen
jokes.explanation: The JFK-spit conspiracy is resolved with a genuine second spitter — Roger McDowell behind the bushes — exactly as Jerry theorized. The fact that there actually WAS a second spitter validates the insane conspiracy theory. The revelation that Newman had poured beer on McDowell's head in the bullpen is the cause that makes the whole absurd chain of events logical. Excellent structural payoff to the episode's best comedic thread.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hello, Pendant Publishing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Pendleton!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Pendant Publishing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Pendleton! Pen-del-ton!

jokes.text: Kramer and Newman reveal they were 'cursing at McDowell in the bullpen all game' and Kramer 'poured a beer on his head'
jokes.setup: Why would McDowell spit on two random fans?
jokes.punchline: They'd been heckling McDowell all game and Kramer literally poured beer on his head — the spit was entirely earned
jokes.explanation: Revealing that Kramer had poured beer on the pitcher's head is the buried cause that makes the whole spit-conspiracy make sense. This detail is brilliantly timed — held back until the very end, making the absurd conspiracy retroactively logical. Newman and Kramer's self-victimization when they literally provoked the attack is classic.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Jerry, what is wrong with you? You can't even use a phone?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? I was trying to—

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This is basic stuff, Jerry. A child could do this.

jokes.text: Newman: 'Maybe because we were sitting in the right-field stands cursing at him in the bullpen all game. He must have caught a glimpse of us when I poured that beer on his head.'
jokes.setup: Kramer asks why McDowell would have targeted them specifically
jokes.punchline: Newman casually reveals they'd been heckling McDowell and he poured a beer on the pitcher's head — buried in the revelation as if it's minor context
jokes.explanation: Newman pouring beer on a pitcher's head while heckling — buried as throwaway context — is a magnificent piece of character work. Newman's matter-of-fact delivery of this escalating detail ('he must have caught a glimpse of us when I poured that beer on his head') is perfectly in character. He's completely unaware this is an extraordinary admission.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's gorgeous! When can I meet her?

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sokol
dialogue.line: Well, she's very busy with her career.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't care! I'll wait! I'll wait years if I have to!

jokes.text: What are you doing? / I gotta move. / You want any help? / I'd love some. You sure? / I'd love to help you. / Yeah, me too!
jokes.setup: After apologizing, Newman and Kramer immediately volunteer to help Keith move
jokes.punchline: Their instantaneous reversal from 'I hate Keith Hernandez / I despise him' to enthusiastically volunteering to help him move, the moment he's cleared of the spit
jokes.explanation: Nice callback payoff. Their five-year hatred of Keith dissolves instantly — and they're so relieved that they immediately volunteer for what Jerry refused to do. The juxtaposition with Jerry's 'it's too soon, I don't know you' refusal is particularly good.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is a cruel joke. You're making fun of me.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Sokol
dialogue.line: No, George. I'm serious. I want to help you. Come live with me.

jokes.text: Elaine on the phone with Jerry after her date: 'You broke up with him?' / Jerry: 'Me too!' — they both dumped Keith for smoking
jokes.setup: Jerry and Elaine both went out with Keith; both have just ended things
jokes.punchline: Both Jerry and Elaine dumped Keith — and both for smoking, as it turns out — simultaneously revealed in parallel phone conversations
jokes.explanation: The parallel dumping — Elaine for the smoking, Jerry for... what exactly? — is a great structural comedy beat. 'Me too!' delivered with equal parts relief and absurdity. The callback to the ashtray observation at 05:29 (Elaine saying she hates cigarettes) is well earned.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's weird about unemployment? It's on your paycheck. Your paycheck! The one thing that's supposed to make you feel good about yourself, and it says 'unemployment' on it. It's like getting a love letter that starts with 'Dear Idiot.'

jokes.text: Jerry desperately chases Keith from his window: 'Keith! Keith! Up here! Could you do me a favor? I need you to come to the unemployment office with me! I'm Jerry's friend! I'm the chucker!'
jokes.setup: George has just missed Keith because Jerry told him Keith already left; George needs Keith to vouch for him at the unemployment office
jokes.punchline: George screaming from Jerry's window, identifying himself to Keith as 'the chucker' — his most defining characteristic in this story — is both desperate and absurd
jokes.explanation: George calling himself 'the chucker' as his identifying credential to Keith Hernandez is a magnificent callback. The desperation of screaming from a window, combined with choosing 'I'm the chucker!' as his introduction, perfectly unites multiple episode threads. One of the episode's great moments.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the problem with unemployment is? It's not the money. It's the psychological impact on your confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You start thinking you're worthless. Like, 'Maybe I AM unemployable.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And then you see a job posting and you're like, 'Oh, I could never do that.' You don't even apply!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like you're already fired from jobs you never had.

jokes.text: What do you need him for? / Keith! Keith! Up here! / Look, could you do me a favor? I need you to go to the unemployment office with me! I'm Jerry's friend! The guy from the locker room! I'm the chucker! It'll take five minutes! Wait! Wait!
jokes.setup: George realizes he has one shot to produce Keith Hernandez for Mrs. Sokol
jokes.punchline: 'I'm the chucker!' — George's final desperate credential to get Keith's attention is to identify himself as the person previously accused of chucking in basketball
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest moments and the greatest callback payoff in the episode. George's desperate identification as 'the chucker' — the thing he was most humiliated by at the episode's opening — as his only possible credential to get Keith's attention is structurally brilliant. The episode opened with the chucker revelation; it closes with George weaponizing the insult as his sole form of introduction. This is what great sitcom writing looks like: a throwaway joke in scene one pays off beautifully in the final act. The escalating desperation ('It'll take five minutes! Wait! Wait!') is also perfectly George.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's ironic? You work your whole life to avoid work. You go to school to get a job so you don't have to work as hard. You get promoted so you can tell other people to work. It's all just work to avoid work.

jokes.text: Jerry's stand-up: the moving-boxes obsession — 'You wander down the street going in and out of stores. Are there boxes here? You can't even talk to people. Shut up! I'm looking for boxes!'
jokes.setup: When you're moving, finding boxes becomes an all-consuming obsession
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'looking for boxes' to yelling 'Shut up! I'm looking for boxes!' captures the absurd single-mindedness perfectly
jokes.explanation: A solid stand-up bit that mirrors the episode's moving subplot. The escalation to 'Shut up! I'm looking for boxes!' is the right comedic peak. The smell-of-cardboard bit that follows extends it well.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I've been putting in a lot of effort trying to avoid work lately. I think I deserve a raise for it.

jokes.text: Stand-up: Moving monologue — 'When you're moving, your whole world becomes boxes...I can smell them!...At a funeral: That's a nice box. Does anybody know where that guy got that box?'
jokes.setup: Moving obsession — the quest for boxes
jokes.punchline: Escalates from normal box-seeking to sniffing out cardboard to coveting a casket at a funeral
jokes.explanation: The box-seeking obsession escalation is genuinely well-crafted stand-up. 'Don't tell me you don't have boxes. Damn it! I can smell them!' is a perfect absurdist escalation. The funeral/casket pivot is dark and earned — 'the last big move of your life' is a strong structural payoff for the whole bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's a perfect oxymoron - 'tremendous job not working'

jokes.text: Jerry's stand-up: 'I love the smell of cardboard in the morning.' — Apocalypse Now parody applied to boxes
jokes.setup: The boxes obsession has intensified; you can now 'smell' boxes
jokes.punchline: Apocalypse Now's 'I love the smell of napalm in the morning' applied to cardboard boxes
jokes.explanation: A good film reference joke. Economy is excellent (one sentence), the build-up earns the parody. Not wildly original but well placed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Lyndon Johnson.

jokes.text: Jerry's stand-up: at a funeral, eyeing the casket: 'That's a nice box. Does anybody know where that guy got that box? When he's done with it, you think I could get that?'
jokes.setup: The boxes obsession now extends to any context
jokes.punchline: Coveting a casket as a moving box at a funeral — death reduced to its utility as excellent packaging
jokes.explanation: The casket-as-moving-box is a brilliantly dark and original extension of the bit. The logic is perfect: it IS a box, it DOES have nice handles. The next step — connecting casket to death as 'the last big move' — sets up the episode's best stand-up conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The baby looks like LBJ.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: What? He does not!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look at him. The ears, the nose, the whole thing. It's LBJ.

jokes.text: Jerry's closing stand-up: 'Death is really the last big move of your life. The hearse is like the van. The pallbearers are close friends. The casket is that great, perfect box. The only problem is once you find it, you're in it.'
jokes.setup: The moving-boxes bit has escalated to the funeral home
jokes.punchline: Death as a 'big move' — complete with the hearse as the moving van, pallbearers as the guys you roped in to help, and the casket as the perfect box — ending with 'the only problem is, once you find it, you're in it'
jokes.explanation: The best stand-up bit in the episode. The extended metaphor of death as 'the last big move' is genuinely philosophical and the execution is nearly flawless. Every element maps: hearse = van, pallbearers = the friends you rope in (callback to the moving subplot), casket = the perfect box. 'The only problem is, once you find it, you're in it' is a perfect dark-comic punchline. This bit transcends simple observational comedy to reach something more resonant. Earns its 9.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about something. There are billions and billions of people in this world, and I'm one of them. And out of all those billions and billions, I can't even get one woman to go out with me.

tmdb_overview: Jerry becomes friends with baseball's Keith Hernandez, who has a history with Kramer and would like a future with Elaine.
insight: Two-part setup introduces George's girlfriend anxiety through 120 jokes in 44 minutes.
